ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists for black pepper beef and crispy fish rice specifically; closest parent 'rice dish' (FOODON_00002809) used as the nearest defensible ancestor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP (rice-based composite dish) is the closest parent category; no exact code exists for this Thai surf-and-turf rice preparation.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept found for this specific Thai mixed rice dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant plate of black pepper beef and crispy fish rice, approximately 400 g, comprising steamed jasmine rice topped with stir-fried black pepper beef slices and deep-fried crispy fish pieces.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Beef, mixed dish with rice and sauce (SR Legacy); Fish, battered/fried (SR Legacy); Jasmine rice, cooked (Foundation Foods) — composite estimation
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for black pepper beef and crispy fish rice exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted composite of three USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy / Foundation Foods entries: (1) beef stir-fry with sauce (~35% by weight, FDC ID 174032 adapted), (2) deep-fried fish fillet (~15% by weight, FDC ID 175177 adapted), and (3) cooked jasmine rice (~50% by weight, FDC ID 169756). Black pepper sauce contribution to sodium and fat is incorporated. Values represent a plausible per-100 g estimate for this Thai restaurant dish.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Jasmine rice, cooked (GI ~68); mixed rice dish with protein and fat (GI reduction estimated to ~62)
gi_source.source_match_notes: Jasmine rice (cooked) has a published GI of approximately 68 (University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson et al., 2021, Diabetes Care). The presence of protein (beef, fish) and fat (frying oil, sauce) in this mixed dish attenuates the glycemic response; a conservative downward adjustment to GI 62 is applied, consistent with published evidence that protein and fat co-ingestion reduces postprandial glucose response (Wolever et al., peer-reviewed literature). Glycemic load per serving = (62 × 82 g carbohydrate per 400 g serving) / 100 = 50.8.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from black pepper sauce, oyster sauce, and soy sauce
hypertension.guidance: At approximately 420 mg sodium per 100 g (1,680 mg per full 400 g serving), this dish provides a substantial portion of the 2,000 mg daily sodium limit recommended for hypertensive individuals; patients should request reduced-sauce preparation or limit portion size to half a plate.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load driven by jasmine rice base
type2_diabetes.guidance: The estimated glycemic load of approximately 50.8 per full serving is high for a single meal; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consider reducing the rice portion to 150–200 g and pairing with additional non-starchy vegetables to blunt postprandial glucose excursion.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate saturated fat from beef and frying oil
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The combination of high sodium (~1,680 mg per serving) and saturated fat (~6.4 g per serving) from beef and deep-frying warrants caution for individuals with cardiovascular disease; choosing lean beef cuts, requesting less sauce, and limiting frequency to occasional consumption is advised.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Black Pepper Beef and Crispy Fish Rice is a Thai-inspired surf-and-turf rice dish served at Thai restaurants in Singapore, notably at Saap Saap Thai. The dish consists of a base of steamed jasmine rice topped with two distinct protein components: tender slices of beef stir-fried in a bold black pepper sauce made with oyster sauce, garlic, and cracked black pepper, and separately deep-fried crispy fish pieces that provide a contrasting golden, crunchy texture. The black pepper sauce delivers a pungent, slightly spicy, and savoury flavour profile characteristic of Thai-Chinese fusion cooking. Nutritionally, the dish is a moderate-energy meal (~672 kcal per 400 g serving) with meaningful protein (~39 g per serving) from both beef and fish, moderate carbohydrate from jasmine rice, and elevated sodium (~1,680 mg per serving) from the sauce components. The glycemic load is high per serving due to the jasmine rice base, making portion control important for individuals managing blood glucose. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians or vegans, contains fish and gluten allergens, and is not certified halal. It is typically consumed as a lunch or dinner main course and represents a popular value-for-money Thai restaurant offering in Singapore's food delivery market.

confidence_reason: No exact match for this dish was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, FAO/INFOODS SEA, or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted composite of USDA SR Legacy and Foundation Foods entries for beef stir-fry, deep-fried fish, and cooked jasmine rice. GI is analogously estimated from published jasmine rice GI with protein/fat attenuation adjustment. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reliably reported across the component USDA source entries used for this composite estimation. 2. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the primary USDA SR Legacy component entries used. 3. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not consistently reported across the composite component sources. 4. The food_group has been reclassified from the input value 'Fish and seafood' to 'Mixed' because this dish contains both beef and fish as co-equal protein components alongside a rice base, making 'Mixed' the more accurate classification. 5. The food_name has been normalised from the menu-style 'RD5 Black Pepper Beef and Crispy Fish Ri' (truncated) to the canonical English name 'Black Pepper Beef and Crispy Fish Rice'. 6. Halal status is marked false as no halal certification information is available for Saap Saap Thai - Funan from approved sources. 7. GI is analogously estimated; a direct GI measurement for this specific dish does not exist in the University of Sydney GI Database. 8. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (62 × 82.0 g carbohydrate in 400 g serving) / 100 = 50.8.
